1: Isaiah 20:1 provides the historical anchor for this chapter: 'In the year that the tartan came to Ashdod, when Sargon king of Assyria sent him.' The tartan (turtanu in Akkadian) was the second-in-command of the Assyrian army, the supreme commander second only to the king. Sargon II (722-705 BCE) is the Assyrian king who captured Samaria (722 BCE) and reorganized the empire after Tiglath-Pileser III and Shalmaneser V. The Ashdod revolt (714-711 BCE): Ashdod (Philistine coastal city) revolted against Assyria, possibly with Egyptian encouragement and backing. The Assyrian annals of Sargon II record the capture of Ashdod and the installation of a new governor. The revolt was supported by the promise of Egyptian military aid — aid that never materialized. This is the specific diplomatic situation Isaiah's sign-act addresses.
2: 'At that time the LORD spoke through Isaiah son of Amoz, saying, Go, take the sackcloth off your waist and remove the sandal from your foot. He obeyed, walking naked and barefoot' — the sign-act (mofet, portent). The sackcloth (saq) was the prophet's distinctive garment (1 Kings 21:27; 2 Kings 1:8; Zechariah 13:4) — possibly Isaiah had been wearing it as a sign of mourning over the coming judgment. The command: remove sackcloth and sandals and walk naked (arom, naked/uncovered) and barefoot (yachef, unshod). Isaiah walked this way for three years — a three-year living prophecy acted out in the streets of Jerusalem. The nakedness signifies: captive status (prisoners were stripped in Assyrian deportations), poverty, and shame. The sandal-removal signifies: captive status (free people wore sandals; slaves and prisoners went barefoot).
3: 'Then the LORD said, Just as my servant Isaiah has walked naked and barefoot for three years as a sign and portent against Egypt and Cush' — YHWH's interpretation of the sign-act: 'ka'asher halakh avdi Yeshayahu arom veyachef shalosch shanim ot umofet al-Mitsrayim ve'al-Kush, as my servant Isaiah has walked naked and barefoot three years as a sign and wonder against Egypt and against Cush.' The 'sign and portent' (ot umofet) language is the Exodus vocabulary — Exodus 7:3 ('I will multiply my signs and wonders in Egypt'); Deuteronomy 6:22 ('YHWH showed signs and wonders against Egypt'). Isaiah is here a living Exodus-type sign: as Moses's signs pointed to Egypt's judgment, Isaiah's naked walk points to Egypt's humiliation.
4: 'So the king of Assyria will lead away the Egyptian captives and the Cushite exiles, young and old alike, naked and barefoot, with their buttocks exposed — Egypt's humiliation laid bare' — the sign's meaning: the Assyrian king leading away Egyptian and Cushite captives (shevi Mitsrayim vegolat Kush, the captivity of Egypt and the exile of Cush). The specific detail: 'young and old, naked and barefoot, with their buttocks exposed' (galui sheth, the buttocks uncovered — the ultimate exposure of shame). Assyrian reliefs from Nineveh and other sites show exactly this: captive populations stripped and marched, sometimes with deliberate exposure of genitalia as an act of degradation. The Cushite pharaohs (25th Dynasty) who seemed the great hope for resistance against Assyria will themselves be stripped and marched. This was partly fulfilled when Esarhaddon conquered Egypt in 671 BCE and Ashurbanipal destroyed Thebes in 663 BCE.
5: 'Those who looked to Cush with expectation and took pride in Egypt will be terrified and put to shame' — the political application to Judah: those who 'looked to Cush with expectation' (vehayu chettim min-Kush, and they will be terrified from Cush = will be terrified about Cush) and those who 'took pride in Egypt' (ufeerim me'Mitsrayim, boasted about Egypt). The political faction in Jerusalem (court officials, merchants, landowners) who wanted to join an anti-Assyrian coalition with Egypt will be 'terrified and ashamed' (vebaioshu, and will be put to shame) when their hoped-for ally is humiliated. 2 Kings 18:19-21 records Rabshakeh's taunt: 'What confidence is this you have? Do you think I am only saying empty words? Look now, you are depending on Egypt, that broken reed of a staff, which pierces the hand of the man who leans on it!'
6: 'In that day the people of this coastland will say, This is what has happened to those we relied on, to whom we fled for help and rescue from the king of Assyria. How then shall we escape?' — the conclusion drawn by 'the people of this coastland' (yoshev haiy hazeh, the inhabitants of this coastland — the Philistine coastal cities: Ashdod, Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron). They had also relied on Egyptian help. The triple reliance: 'relied on' (mavtachenu), 'fled for help' (nasa leshavar, where we fled for help), and 'rescue from the king of Assyria' (lehinnatseil mippanei melech Ashshur, to be rescued from before the king of Assyria). All three expectations are shattered. 'How then shall we escape?' (ve'anachnu eikh nimmalet, and we — how will we escape?) — the rhetorical question that has no human answer. Only YHWH can provide escape (cf. 20's subtext: trust YHWH alone).
